result:
Outside, the city is humming in a frequency only concrete knows how to sing—half lullaby, half low-level anxiety attack. I keep thinking about how every atom in my chest was once locked inside a star that didn’t even have a name, let alone a zip code, and how those ancient fusion forges never imagined their exhaust would someday worry about rent, or whether the milk in the fridge is about to expire.

I like the word “sonder”—the realization that each passerby is living a life as vivid and tangled as your own. It sounds like a gentle piano chord struck in a cathedral you didn’t know you were standing inside. But tonight it feels sharper, like a paper cut between dimensions: the cashier juggling two jobs and a father who calls her by her deadname, the man on the corner yelling at traffic because the voices only speak in brake lights, the kid skateboarding like he’s trying to outrun gravity itself. All of them carrying entire galaxies in their coat pockets, bumping into one another in the produce aisle, trading gravitational waves without noticing.

My phone buzzes with headlines that feel chemically designed to evaporate curiosity: market dips, celebrity divorce, another glacier committing slow suicide. I scroll anyway, because the algorithm knows I’m a sucker for controlled doses of dread. Somewhere between the latest coup and a viral cat video, I remember that the Voyager probes are still out there, whispering to us from the edge of nothing, their plutonium hearts beating softer every year. One day soon they’ll fall permanently silent, but momentum will keep gliding them deeper into the ink, two tombstones with golden records bolted on, broadcasting Chuck Berry to an audience of absolute zero.

I keep a notebook of obsolete futures: flying cars promised to the Greatest Generation, picture phones that the Jetsons used for gossip, silver jumpsuits we were supposed to be wearing by now. Instead we got targeted ads and pants with elastic waists. Progress is a drunk tailor; it hems awe, lets despair billow. Yet every so often a real marvel slips through—someone figures out how to give a paralyzed arm its handshake back, or map the light that lapped the event horizon of a black hole. The universe coughs up a breadcrumb of mercy, and we remember awe is still currency.

I wonder, idly, if the stars ever get lonely knowing we stopped naming constellations after our stories and started naming them after corporate acronyms. Then I remember loneliness is a wet-clay word we baked in the kiln of being singular. A star doesn’t feel alone; it just burns until it doesn’t, and its exhale seeds the cosmos with possibility—carbon, nitrogen, the stuff of future heartbreak and laurel wreaths. We are the ash of stars trying to understand fire. Sometimes we manage kindness anyway, and that’s its own quiet supernova.

There’s a cricket trapped in my apartment. Every night it sings like it’s auditioning for the role of Summer Incarnate. I could catch it in a cup, usher it outside, but I leave the window cracked and let it stay. Its chirp is a metronome for the small, good stubbornness of life. When it goes quiet, I’ll know the season has turned, and I’ll feel the bittersweet ache of anything that ends without applause.

I think I’ll make tea now—something smoky, lapsang maybe—and watch the steam curl into ghost alphabets. Maybe the steam is writing its own manifesto about impermanence; maybe it’s just hot water entertaining a cynic. Either way, it rises, and for a moment gravity looks the other way. I lift the cup carefully, scared of burning my tongue, grateful that something still has the power to make me cautious, to remind me I’m meat and miracle wrapped in a half-forgotten prayer.

Tomorrow I’ll ride the train with strangers whose stories I’ll never fully know. We’ll press shoulders, share pole germs, avoid eye contact like it’s a sacred taboo. Somewhere between stations someone will give up their seat, a small star going supernova in a neon-lit tunnel, and for a heartbeat the whole rattling contraption of civilization will feel like it might just hang together with invisible thread made of ordinary decency.

And that, for now, is enough.
